one_liner: Electron + Vue 3 desktop CLIENT for offline talking-head avatar video generation: it clones a real person's appearance and voice from an uploaded video, then drives a lip-synced avatar from text/audio. All actual AI (lip-sync video-gen, TTS voice-clone, ASR) runs in three separate GPU-only Docker containers that the client calls over localhost HTTP; no ML/model source code lives in this repo.
language_runtime: Node.js 18 / Electron ^33 (electron-vite ^2.3, Vue ^3.5.13, Pinia, vue-router, tdesign-vue-next, better-sqlite3, fluent-ffmpeg, axios). Backend services are Python+PyTorch but shipped ONLY as prebuilt Docker images (guiji2025/duix.avatar, guiji2025/fish-speech-ziming, guiji2025/fun-asr) — their source is NOT in this repo.
license: Duix.com Community License (per the LICENSE file, not a badge). Source-available, Llama-style — NOT MIT/Apache. Commercial trigger at greater than 1,000 Monthly Active Users requires a separate paid commercial license from Duix.com. Mandatory "Built with DUIX.COM" attribution, a Notice text file, and a "DUIX.COM" prefix on any derived model name. Two separate model-licensing-agreement PDFs are also bundled. (README text contradicts the LICENSE, citing 100k users / $10M — the binding LICENSE file says 1 thousand MAU.)

README requires RTX 4070 / 32GB RAM / 100GB+30GB disk and states the three services will not start without an NVIDIA card. The Electron client is CPU-light, but useless without the GPU backend. Direct violation of ClipPilot's CPU-only-MVP constraint.

one_liner: The canonical FFmpeg multimedia framework: C libraries (libavcodec/libavformat/libavfilter/libavutil/libswscale/libswresample) plus the ffmpeg/ffprobe/ffplay CLI tools for decoding, encoding, trimming, scaling, filtering, captioning, muxing and analyzing audio/video. This is the core media-processing engine, not a Python package.
language_runtime: C (C99/C11). Builds native ffmpeg.exe/ffprobe.exe/ffplay.exe via ./configure + make. No Python API or bindings in this tree; only Python files are tools/python/tf_sess_config.py (TensorFlow session-config helper) and tools/normalize.py. ClipPilot (Python+PySide6) invokes the compiled binaries via subprocess, not as a library.
license: LGPL v2.1-or-later by default (per LICENSE.md and COPYING.LGPLv2.1). Becomes GPL v2+ only if built with --enable-gpl, which activates GPL-only filters (vf_cropdetect.c, vf_delogo.c, vf_mpdecimate.c, vf_signature.c, vf_blackframe.c) and libx264/x265. A few MIT/BSD/libjpeg files. NOT AGPL.
compute: CPU-only by default. Optional GPU accel (NVENC/CUDA, QSV, Vulkan, vf_thumbnail_cuda.cu) is compile/runtime-optional and not required. Bundle a CPU-only prebuilt static Windows binary (gyan.dev or BtbN build) for the MVP.

understanding_capability: Scene/shot-change detection: libavfilter/f_select.c (select='gt(scene,0.4)') backed by scene_sad.c (sum-of-absolute-difference between frames) - works with NO external deps, gives shot boundaries & cut frequency (pacing)
Black-frame / freeze / silence detection: vf_blackdetect.c, vf_freezedetect.c, af_silencedetect.c - segment boundaries and dead-air, all built-in
Thumbnail/representative-frame selection: vf_thumbnail.c (and vf_thumbnail_cuda) - picks the most representative frame per window for visual summary
Crop/letterbox detection: vf_cropdetect.c (GPL) - detect active region / aspect for safe reframe to 9:16
Quality signals: vf_blurdetect.c, vf_blockdetect.c, vf_colordetect.c, vf_edgedetect.c - visual quality/sharpness cues
On-screen text OCR: vf_ocr.c (requires <tesseract/capi.h>, --enable-libtesseract, default OFF) - reads burned-in captions/titles

clippilot_role: Core media-processing backbone for the entire pipeline. ffmpeg.exe drives the clip, caption, compose, and (audio) transcode stages; ffprobe.exe (especially -of json) drives ingest metadata. For the NEW holistic-understanding feature it is the frame-extraction + cheap-signal layer: built-in scene/black/freeze/silence/thumbnail/loudness filters give Claude pacing, shot boundaries, energy, and representative frames at zero extra cost - which Claude/other models (Whisper ASR, CLIP/face models) then reason over. It is NOT itself a semantic understanding engine (no faces/emotion/ASR).
reason: Non-negotiable foundation: it is the only tool that does the clip/reframe/caption/compose/normalize work AND, via built-in scene/silence/black/thumbnail/loudness filters, supplies the cheap visual/audio signals + frame extraction that the holistic-understanding feature feeds to Claude and perception models. CPU-only, Windows-native, subprocess-friendly, LGPL-clean if you avoid GPL filters.
gotchas: Do NOT vendor/compile this 1.8GB+ C source tree - it has no prebuilt binary, no Python binding, and building on Windows needs MSYS2/MinGW toolchain. Ship a prebuilt CPU-only static ffmpeg/ffprobe .exe (gyan.dev or BtbN) instead.

License gotcha: stay on the default LGPL build. --enable-gpl pulls in GPL filters (cropdetect, delogo, mpdecimate, signature) and libx264/x265, flipping the whole binary to GPL v2+ - which can force source-disclosure obligations on a commercial ClipPilot. If you need cropdetect/x264, that is the trigger to evaluate; many distributions ship GPL builds, so verify which build you bundle.

OCR (vf_ocr) is OFF by default and needs --enable-libtesseract + tessdata models - standard Windows builds do NOT include it. Either build a custom binary or run OCR via a separate Python lib (pytesseract/easyocr).

DNN object/scene detection (vf_dnn_detect/dnn_classify) is OFF by default and needs --enable-libtensorflow / --enable-libopenvino / --enable-libtorch plus model files - not in standard builds and OpenVINO/Torch are heavy. Prefer a dedicated Python perception repo over FFmpeg's DNN path.

No ASR, no face detection, no emotion, no speaker diarization anywhere in FFmpeg - the holistic-understanding feature still needs separate repos for transcript/faces/emotion; FFmpeg only provides frames + visual/audio signals.

ffprobe/ffmpeg report many analysis results (scene scores, silencedetect, blackdetect, loudnorm stats) to stderr or as frame metadata, NOT clean JSON - ClipPilot must parse stderr/showinfo text or use -of json only where ffprobe supports it. Plan robust subprocess output parsing.
Master branch (8.0.git) is a moving target; pin to a tagged release build for reproducibility rather than tracking master.
Heavy CPU for re-encode: -c copy is instant but any filter (scale/subtitles/loudnorm) forces a full re-encode - budget CPU time and consider -preset/-threads tuning on the CPU-only MVP.
key_deps: Build toolchain: GNU Make 3.81+, C compiler (MSYS2/MinGW on Windows), nasm/yasm for asm
Optional external libs are OFF by default per INSTALL.md: libx264/libx265 (GPL), libtesseract (OCR), libtensorflow/libopenvino/libtorch (DNN), libvpx, libfdk-aac (nonfree)
Runtime deps for a prebuilt static binary: none (self-contained .exe)

one_liner: Language-agnostic subtitle-to-video TIME synchronizer: extracts a speech/silence signal from the video's audio track via VAD (webrtc/silero/auditok/fused) or embedded/PGS subtitle timings, then FFT cross-correlates it against an input .srt to find the offset (and optional framerate scale) and writes a re-timed subtitle file. It corrects subtitle drift; it does NOT transcribe or understand video content.
language_runtime: Python 3.6-3.14 (per setup.py classifiers); pure-Python orchestration shelling out to ffmpeg/ffprobe binaries.
license: MIT (LICENSE file: "Copyright 2019 Stephen Macke ... Permission is hereby granted, free of charge ..."). NOTE: the optional `auditok==0.1.5` VAD backend is GPLv3 — the code itself (speech_transformers._make_auditok_detector) prints a warning that importing it at runtime creates a GPLv3 derivative work. Default VAD is webrtc, so auditok is avoidable.
compute: CPU-only. webrtcvad/auditok are CPU; silero VAD pulls optional torch (CPU is fine, no GPU/CUDA required). No GPU code anywhere. The heavy lifting is an ffmpeg subprocess (audio decode) + numpy FFT.

ffsubsync.make_parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser for programmatic arg construction
ffsubsync.ffsubsync.extract_subtitles_from_reference (via --extract-subs-from-stream) — rips an embedded text subtitle track to .srt using ffmpeg

Notable flags: --vad {webrtc,silero,auditok,fused,...}, --multi-segment-sync/--segment-count, --pgs-ref-stream (Blu-ray bitmap-sub timings), --skip-sync-on-low-quality (+--min-score/--quality-max-offset-seconds), --apply-offset-seconds, --gss, --reference-stream, --max-duration-seconds, remote-URL references
understanding_capability: Voice Activity Detection (speech vs non-speech) on the audio track only — webrtcvad (webrtcvad-wheels), silero-vad (torch.hub snakers4/silero-vad), auditok energy-based, and a fused webrtc+silero detector (weighted/intersection/union). Output is a per-frame 0/1 speech signal, NOT transcribed text.
Embedded subtitle-stream enumeration/extraction via ffprobe/ffmpeg (text tracks -> srt; speech timing only).
PGS/Blu-ray bitmap-subtitle on-screen timing recovery from MKV packet PTS via ffprobe (when-captions-are-shown, no bitmap decode/OCR).
Coarse non-dialogue cue detection in subtitle text (_is_metadata: music-note symbols, bracketed [music], '<i>' markup) — text heuristic, not perception.
generation_capability: None. Produces only a re-timed subtitle file (offset + optional framerate scale applied to an EXISTING .srt). No video, audio, image, caption-text, or transcript generation.
clippilot_role: Optional QA/polish utility in the caption->compose stage: after ClipPilot generates burned-in or sidecar captions, ffsubsync can re-sync a drifted/imported .srt to the clip's audio so captions land on the right words. Useful in Section A (paid clipping/DFY) when a client supplies their own out-of-sync subtitle file, or when re-using third-party sourced footage that ships an .srt that needs realigning. It is NOT a transcriber and contributes ZERO to the new holistic video-understanding requirement (no scenes/faces/OCR/emotion/music-energy; only speech-vs-silence on audio).
reason: Clean MIT, Windows-native, CPU-only, importable run() with a progress_handler callback — a great drop-in for the niche "re-sync an existing/imported subtitle file" job, but it solves a problem ClipPilot mostly avoids (its own ASR like Whisper already yields word-aligned timings), and it provides none of the required holistic video-understanding.
gotchas: Does NOT transcribe or understand video — only aligns an EXISTING subtitle file's timing. If ClipPilot's transcribe step (e.g. Whisper) already emits word/segment timestamps, ffsubsync is redundant; it's only valuable for externally-supplied/drifted .srt inputs.
Hard external dependency on ffmpeg AND ffprobe binaries on PATH (ffmpeg_utils.ffmpeg_bin_path appends '.exe' on Windows) — ClipPilot must bundle/locate them; not a pip dep.

auditok==0.1.5 VAD backend is GPLv3 (code self-warns importing it creates a GPLv3 derivative). For the commercial DFY/ad-share sections, avoid `--vad auditok`; stick to the MIT/BSD webrtc default or silero.
auditok pinned to ancient ==0.1.5 (auditok API changed substantially in 0.2+); that backend may be brittle on modern installs.

silero/fused VAD require optional torch (extras_require 'torch'); first run does torch.hub.load(snakers4/silero-vad) which downloads the model from the network at runtime — offline/sandboxed environments will fail. Default webrtc avoids torch entirely.

Calls subprocess + reads ffmpeg stdout pipes; on Windows it uses STARTUPINFO/STARTF_USESHOWWINDOW (handled), but ClipPilot must not run it on the GUI thread (blocking). The run() progress_handler callback is the right hook for a PySide6 worker thread.
GUI here is Gooey/wxPython/PyInstaller (gui/requirements.txt) — irrelevant and incompatible with the PySide6 app; ignore the gui/ folder, use the library/CLI only.
pyproject.toml is dynamic/sparse (metadata comes from setup.py + requirements.txt); install via the package, not by reading pyproject alone.

license: MIT - confirmed by reading the LICENSE file (Copyright (c) 2024 FujiwaraChoki), not just a badge. Clean and commercial-friendly.
compute: CPU-only. No torch / CUDA / GPU code anywhere (verified by grep + uv.lock). Heavy work is MoviePy + FFmpeg subprocess + ImageMagick (CPU libx264 encode, preset=medium). LLM is offloaded to a separate local Ollama server process; ASR is optional AssemblyAI CLOUD API. External deps required: a running Ollama, FFmpeg on PATH, ImageMagick on PATH, Pexels API key, TikTok sessionid.

clippilot_role: Weak fit for ClipPilot's locked architecture, and ZERO fit for the new holistic video-understanding requirement. ClipPilot is INPUT-video-driven (ingest -> transcribe -> find_highlights -> clip); MoneyPrinter is OUTPUT-video-from-text (no ingest, no perception of any uploaded media at all). The only reusable IDEAS are patterns ClipPilot's Phase 0 already has or does better: (1) the Flask-API + decoupled polling worker + DB job-queue with cancel/event-log (main.py + worker.py + repository.py) mirrors ClipPilot's existing SQLite state machine - reference, do not import; (2) the MoviePy 9:16 crop+resize+subtitle-burn recipe in video.py (combine_videos / generate_video) is a clean, copy-able reference for the clip/caption/compose stages; (3) ollama Client usage in gpt.py is a tidy local-LLM call pattern. Nothing here helps Section A clipping, and the faceless Section B funnel would be better served by purpose-built clip-from-source logic than by this Pexels-stitching approach.
reason: It is a text-to-Shorts generator with literally zero video-understanding/perception code (no ASR-of-input, scene/shot detect, OCR, face/emotion, audio-energy, or CLIP) and a different I/O model than ClipPilot's ingest-driven pipeline; keep only as a MoviePy 9:16-compose + worker-queue code reference.
key_modules: Backend/video.py (MoviePy 9:16 crop/concat, subtitle burn, AssemblyAI/local SRT)
key_deps: external runtime: FFmpeg, ImageMagick, a running Ollama server
gotchas: NO video-understanding capability whatsoever - cannot satisfy the new holistic-understanding feature; would need a completely separate perception stack (whisper/scenedetect/CLIP/OCR/face).
Inverted I/O model: generates videos from a TEXT subject; it never ingests or analyzes a user-uploaded video, so it does not map onto ClipPilot's ingest->transcribe->find_highlights flow.

AssemblyAI subtitles are CLOUD (paid API key) and only ever transcribe the TTS audio it generated itself, not any source video; the local fallback just slices per-sentence TTS durations (no real ASR).

TTS depends on TikTok's undocumented private voice endpoint + a scraped TIKTOK_SESSION_ID cookie (tiktokvoice.py) - fragile, ToS-gray, breaks without notice; bad for a guardrails-on commercial product.

Hard external toolchain: requires FFmpeg + ImageMagick on PATH AND a separately running Ollama server (gpt.py talks to localhost:11434); check_env_vars() sys.exit(1) if Pexels/TikTok keys or ImageMagick missing.
pillow pinned to 9.5.0 (old) and playsound==1.2.2 is a known wheel-build pain on Windows (README even documents the workaround).
Default deployment is Docker+Postgres (docker-compose), not a native PySide6 app; running it as-is means standing up a Flask server + worker + DB rather than importing functions.
video.py burns hardcoded styling (font_size=100, yellow, fonts/bold_font.ttf) - not parameterized for ClipPilot's caption needs.
pipeline.py does os-level taskkill /f /im ffmpeg.exe on Windows after each render - would kill ANY other ffmpeg ClipPilot has running.
Pexels-only sourcing with no licensing/attribution guardrails; third-party-sourcing compliance is on the caller.
README states 'Pull Requests will not be accepted' - upstream is effectively closed to contributions.

one_liner: A Streamlit GUI app that GENERATES faceless short videos: LLM writes a script -> cloud/local TTS dubs it -> stock clips (Pexels/Pixabay) or Stable Diffusion images are fetched -> FFmpeg normalizes/concats/adds transitions+bg-music -> ASR (faster-whisper/SenseVoice/cloud) burns subtitles -> Selenium auto-publishes to Chinese platforms. It is a generation/assembly pipeline, NOT a video-understanding tool.
language_runtime: Python 3 (Streamlit 1.34 web app, launched via `streamlit run gui.py`). Not a CLI, not PySide6. Cross-platform Python but FFmpeg/FFprobe must be on PATH; Selenium needs Chrome.
license: CONTRADICTORY/DUAL. LICENSE file is full GNU GPL-3.0 text, but a separate `Copyright` file AND a header in EVERY .py file state: "personal and educational use only. Commercial use is strictly prohibited unless explicit permission is obtained from the author." The non-commercial header overrides any GPL grant in practice.
compute: CPU-only works for the MVP path (cloud LLM + cloud TTS + faster-whisper on CPU via compute_type=int8). GPU optional and only if you self-host: faster-whisper CUDA, ChatTTS/CosyVoice/GPTSoVITS local TTS (torch==2.3.1), and Stable Diffusion (expects an EXTERNAL AUTOMATIC1111 webui server — not bundled).

one_liner: Pure-Python video editing/compositing library that orchestrates FFmpeg via subprocess pipes: load clips, cut/concatenate/composite, apply effects, burn in captions/subtitles, and render the final MP4/GIF. It is an assembly/render engine, not a perception engine.
language_runtime: Python >=3.9 (classifiers cover 3.9-3.12); pure Python with no compiled extensions of its own. Shells out to an FFmpeg binary via subprocess.
license: MIT License, per LICENCE.txt ("Copyright (c) 2015 Zulko", full MIT text) and pyproject license={text="MIT License"}. Permissive, commercial-safe.
compute: CPU-only. No CUDA/torch/tensorflow/onnx in moviepy/ (grep clean); numpy frame math runs on CPU. Only GPU touchpoint is an optional ffmpeg 'h264_nvenc' codec string passed through to FFmpeg in ffmpeg_writer.py lines 137/158 -- opt-in encoder, not required; default libx264 is CPU.
entry_points: No CLI / no console_scripts / no __main__ / no server -- library only (pyproject has no [project.scripts]).

Public API: `from moviepy import *` defined in moviepy/__init__.py (__all__ lists VideoFileClip, AudioFileClip, ImageClip, ColorClip, TextClip, CompositeVideoClip, concatenate_videoclips, clips_array, ImageSequenceClip, vfx, afx, videotools, ffmpeg_tools, convert_to_seconds).
moviepy/config.py check() (run as `python -m moviepy.config`) self-tests for ffmpeg/ffplay binaries.
moviepy/video/io/ffmpeg_tools.py free functions: ffmpeg_extract_subclip, ffmpeg_merge_video_audio, ffmpeg_extract_audio, ffmpeg_resize, ffmpeg_stabilize_video, ffmpeg_copy, ffmpeg_version.
callable_surface: VideoFileClip(path).subclipped(t1,t2) -- load + trim a source clip
clip.with_effects([vfx.Resize(...), vfx.Crop(...), vfx.MultiplySpeed(...)]) -- apply video FX (vfx package: Resize, Crop, Rotate, FadeIn/Out, CrossFadeIn/Out, Margin, MirrorX/Y, etc.)
CompositeVideoClip([base_clip, TextClip(...).with_position(...)]) -- overlay captions/graphics
concatenate_videoclips([...]) / clips_array([[...]]) -- stitch/compose
clip.write_videofile(out, codec='libx264', fps=...) -- final render (FFMPEG_VideoWriter)
TextClip(font=..., text=..., font_size=..., color=..., stroke_color=..., stroke_width=...) -- caption rendering via Pillow
moviepy.video.tools.subtitles.SubtitlesClip(srt, make_textclip=...) + file_to_subtitles() -- burn-in .srt captions
ffmpeg_tools.ffmpeg_extract_subclip(infile,t1,t2,outfile) -- stream-copy lossless cut (no re-encode)
ffmpeg_tools.ffmpeg_extract_audio(infile, outfile) -- pull audio track out for an external ASR step
moviepy.video.io.ffmpeg_reader.ffmpeg_parse_infos(path) -- probe duration/fps/size/codec/rotation/bitrate/metadata
understanding_capability: Luminosity-based scene/shot cut detection: moviepy/video/tools/cuts.py detect_scenes(clip, luminosity_threshold=10) returns cut boundaries [(0,t1),(t1,t2)...] from per-frame luminosity jumps. Crude (brightness-only), CPU heavy (iterates every frame), but a real, dependency-free shot-boundary signal.

Frame self-similarity matching: cuts.py FramesMatches.from_clip(clip, distance_threshold, max_duration) + select_scenes() finds visually-similar frame pairs (Euclidean distance on flattened RGB) -- intended for GIF looping, repurposable as a loop/repeated-shot detector.
Video period/loop detection: cuts.py find_video_period() via frame correlation.
Audio period/energy: audio/tools/cuts.py find_audio_period() (autocorrelation of per-chunk volume) and AudioClip.to_soundarray() give raw RMS/energy arrays usable as a crude pacing/beat proxy.
Metadata probing: ffmpeg_reader.ffmpeg_parse_infos() returns duration, fps, resolution, codec, rotation, bitrate, container metadata.
Raw frame access: clip.iter_frames()/get_frame(t) yields numpy RGB arrays -- the hand-off point to feed an external vision model (OpenCV is already a dep).
generation_capability: Subclip extraction / trimming (subclipped, ffmpeg_extract_subclip)
Concatenation and grid/composite layout (concatenate_videoclips, clips_array, CompositeVideoClip)
Vertical 9:16 reframing via vfx.Crop + vfx.Resize (Shorts/Reels format)
Caption/title rendering and burn-in (TextClip, SubtitlesClip -> CompositeVideoClip)
Video FX: fades, crossfades, speed change, rotate, mirror, margins, color ops (vfx package)
Audio FX + mixing: AudioFadeIn/Out, AudioNormalize, MultiplyVolume, CompositeAudioClip (afx package)
Final encode to MP4/WebM/GIF (write_videofile, write_gif), merge separate video+audio (ffmpeg_merge_video_audio)
Resize/stabilize/remux helpers (ffmpeg_resize, ffmpeg_stabilize_video deshake filter, ffmpeg_copy)
clippilot_role: Primary Python in-process clip/caption/compose engine for the pipeline's clip -> caption -> compose stages. ClipPilot (Python+PySide6) imports moviepy directly: VideoFileClip.subclipped() to cut highlights, vfx.Crop/Resize to reframe to 9:16, TextClip/SubtitlesClip+CompositeVideoClip to burn captions, concatenate/CompositeVideoClip to assemble, write_videofile() to render the approval-gate preview. Use ffmpeg_extract_subclip for fast lossless cuts and ffmpeg_extract_audio to feed the external ASR (transcribe) stage. It is NOT the holistic-understanding engine -- only luminosity scene-cuts + frame-distance matching exist; real perception (ASR, object/face/scene recognition, OCR, emotion, CLIP/embeddings) must come from other repos, with moviepy supplying frame numpy arrays (iter_frames) and shot boundaries (detect_scenes) as input to them.
reason: De-facto standard MIT, CPU-only, Windows-native Python editing library that cleanly covers ClipPilot's clip/caption/compose/render stages as an in-process import; mature and actively maintained (v2.2.0).
key_deps: imageio_ffmpeg>=0.2.0 (bundles the ffmpeg binary -> no system install needed on Windows)
opencv-python-headless>=4.0.0 (already present -> can host an external vision step)
key_modules: moviepy/video/tools/cuts.py (detect_scenes, FramesMatches -- the only visual 'understanding' code)
gotchas: NOT a perception/understanding engine: only luminosity-threshold scene cuts (detect_scenes) and RGB-distance frame matching exist. No ASR, object/scene/face recognition, OCR, emotion, music analysis, or CLIP/embeddings. Holistic understanding must be sourced elsewhere; moviepy only supplies frames + shot boundaries.
detect_scenes() and FramesMatches.from_clip() decode and iterate EVERY frame in pure Python/numpy -- O(n) per frame and slow on long uploads; fine for short clips, budget time for full videos on CPU.
subtitles.py is labeled 'Experimental module' and file_to_subtitles only parses .srt (no .vtt/.ass).

TextClip needs a font file path (SubtitlesClip raises ValueError if font is None) -- ClipPilot must ship/point to a .ttf; historic ImageMagick dependency is gone in v2 (now Pillow), but watch for font-path issues on Windows.

Relies on an ffmpeg binary: defaults to imageio-ffmpeg's bundled exe, else auto-detects ffmpeg/ffmpeg.exe on PATH (config.py). Pin/verify imageio_ffmpeg so the bundled binary is present; FFMPEG_BINARY can be overridden via .env.
h264_nvenc codec is a user-supplied passthrough in ffmpeg_writer.py -- only works if the FFmpeg build + NVIDIA GPU support it; keep MVP on default libx264 (CPU).
MoviePy v2 is a breaking API rewrite vs v1 (method renames like subclip->subclipped, fx moved to with_effects([...])). Pin to v2.x and ignore v1-era tutorials/snippets.
Clips hold open ffmpeg subprocess pipes/file handles; must call clip.close() (or use context managers) to avoid leaked processes/locked files on Windows in a long-running PySide6 app.

one_liner: An Electron + Next.js/React (Vite) desktop "generative AI studio" UI that is almost entirely a thin client to the hosted muapi.ai REST API (submit -> poll) for image/video/lipsync/audio/clipping generation. The only genuinely local capability is text-to-IMAGE generation via a bundled stable-diffusion.cpp sd-cli binary, plus an optional user-hosted Wan2GP Gradio bridge for local video gen. It does NO local video understanding.
language_runtime: JavaScript/TypeScript on Node.js 20 (Electron ^33.4.11, Next.js ^15, React ^19, Vite ^5). NOT Python — no requirements.txt/pyproject/.py files exist. Local image gen shells out to a native C++ binary (stable-diffusion.cpp sd-cli). Cloud path is pure HTTPS fetch.
license: MIT — root LICENSE file ("Copyright (c) 2026 Open Generative AI Contributors"); package.json also declares "license":"MIT". Caveat: 3 git submodules (Vibe-Workflow, Open-Poe-AI, Open-AI-Design-Agent per .gitmodules) are separate repos with unverified licenses, and downloaded model weights (SDXL, DreamShaper, Realistic Vision, Z-Image, Qwen3) each carry their own non-MIT licenses.
compute: CPU-only works for the muapi.ai cloud path (all heavy compute is server-side). Local sd.cpp image gen is CPU-capable but slow; GPU/Metal accel in this repo's custom binary (CUSTOM_BINARIES map in localInference.js) is macOS-arm64 ONLY. Local VIDEO gen requires the user to run Wan2GP separately on an NVIDIA CUDA box (wan2gpProvider.js explicitly references a "dedicated CUDA box"). No bundled GPU video gen.

It is a competitor-style front-end, not a library: there is no Python, no reusable backend, and the actual clipping/highlight + all generation logic lives behind the closed muapi.ai API (paid, x-api-key). For ClipPilot the only transferable assets are (a) the muapi.ai API contract itself — the 'ai-clipping' endpoint shape (video_url, num_highlights, aspect_ratio, return_coordinates_only -> clips or {label,start,end,score}) which ClipPilot's find_highlights/clip stages could call directly from Python with requests/httpx if you choose to rent muapi as a cloud provider; and (b) reference patterns for the sd.cpp subprocess wrapper and the Wan2GP Gradio bridge. It contributes NOTHING to the new holistic video-understanding requirement.
reason: JS/Electron thin client with no local intelligence — clipping and all gen are behind the paid closed muapi.ai API; zero video-understanding/perception code; nothing importable into a Python+PySide6 app.
gotchas: NO video-understanding/perception anywhere: grep for whisper/asr/transcribe/scene-detect/shot-detect/object/face-detect/emotion/ocr/embedding/clip returns only GENERATION model params (face-swap, scene relight, TTS emotion/whisper voices in models_dump.json), not analysis. Useless for the holistic-understanding feature.
Wrong stack for ClipPilot: pure JS/TS + Electron, no Python, no PySide6 — cannot import as a library; would require subprocess or HTTP only.

The 'AI Clipping Studio' (packages/studio/src/components/ClippingStudio.jsx) is just UI; the real highlight logic is the remote muapi.ai /api/v1/ai-clipping endpoint (runClipping in muapi.js) — a paid third-party closed API requiring an x-api-key, not self-hostable.
ClippingStudio mocks fake coordinates client-side when the API returns none (lines ~357-366) — misleading 'it works' impression.

Local GPU acceleration is macOS-arm64 ONLY (CUSTOM_BINARIES in localInference.js); on Windows sd.cpp runs CPU-only and slow. Local VIDEO gen needs a separate NVIDIA CUDA box running Wan2GP (violates CPU-only MVP).
electron/main.js sets webPreferences.webSecurity:false — security smell to avoid copying.
Depends on git submodules (Vibe-Workflow, Open-Poe-AI, Open-AI-Design-Agent) not vetted here; their licenses/availability are unverified. npm run setup requires submodule init.
Downloaded weights via hardcoded HuggingFace URLs (modelCatalog.js) carry their own licenses (e.g. SDXL CreativeML OpenRAIL) — separate commercial review needed if you ever reused this path.

All cloud calls hardcode base https://api.muapi.ai with x-api-key in localStorage; vendor lock-in and a per-call billed dependency (getUserBalance, calculateDynamicCost endpoints confirm metered paid usage).

one_liner: Agent-first open-source video production framework (the AI coding assistant IS the orchestrator; there is no code orchestrator). It exposes a registry of ~90 Python BaseTool subclasses (video/image/TTS/music generation + analysis/perception + FFmpeg/Remotion/HyperFrames compose), driven by YAML pipeline manifests and Markdown director skills. For ClipPilot the gold is tools/analysis/: video_analyzer.py fuses transcript + scenes + keyframes + audio-energy + faces into one VideoAnalysisBrief, and video_understand.py runs CLIP/BLIP-2/LLaVA on frames.
language_runtime: Python >=3.10 (setup.py python_requires=">=3.10"). Node.js >=18 (Remotion) / >=22 (HyperFrames) only for the compose/render layer, NOT for the analysis tools ClipPilot would reuse. FFmpeg is a hard external-binary dependency for most analysis tools (declared as cmd:ffmpeg in each tool's .dependencies).
license: GNU AGPLv3 - confirmed by reading the LICENSE file line 1 ("GNU AFFERO GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE Version 3, 19 November 2007"), not the README badge. Strong network-copyleft.
compute: CPU-only works for the highest-value perception tools: video_analyzer (runtime=LOCAL), transcriber (faster-whisper CPU, vram_mb=0), scene_detect (PySceneDetect/ffmpeg), audio_energy (ffmpeg ebur128), face_tracker (MediaPipe with OpenCV-Haar fallback), frame_sampler, clip_embedder ViT-B/32 (~150-300ms/img on CPU per module docstring), visual_qa. NVIDIA GPU optional: only needed to make video_understand's BLIP-2/LLaVA practical (CLIP path runs on CPU) and for local video/image GENERATION (WAN/Hunyuan/CogVideo/LTX/local diffusion) which are out of ClipPilot CPU-only MVP scope. video_understand declares runtime=LOCAL_GPU vram_mb=2048; requirements-gpu.txt = torch/torchaudio/torchvision only.
entry_points: No argparse/__main__/CLI binary, no HTTP server, no pyproject entry-points. Entry is the importable registry singleton.

tools/analysis/video_analyzer.py :: VideoAnalyzer.execute({'source': <path|url>, 'analysis_depth': 'standard'|'deep'})  -- the holistic-understanding orchestrator
tools/analysis/video_understand.py :: VideoUnderstand.execute({'input_path','mode':'describe|qa|quality|classify','model':'clip|blip2|llava'})
lib/clip_embedder.py :: embed_images() / embed_texts() / pool_frames()  -- module-level CLIP funcs for highlight/visual ranking

Makefile targets: make setup / make preflight / make test-contracts

Music/SFX: Suno, ElevenLabs, Pixabay, Freesound (tools/audio/)
Composition/render: Remotion (React, word-level captions/TalkingHead), HyperFrames (HTML/GSAP), FFmpeg compose/stitch/trim
Subtitles: subtitle_gen.py (SRT/VTT, word-level)
Enhancement: upscale (Real-ESRGAN), bg_remove (rembg/U2Net), face_restore (CodeFormer/GFPGAN), color_grade (GPU/heavy)
clippilot_role: Reference donor for ClipPilot's NEW holistic-understanding stage and for clip mechanics. ClipPilot is Python+PySide6, so the BaseTool perception tools (video_analyzer, transcriber, scene_detect, audio_energy, face_tracker, frame_sampler, visual_qa, lib/clip_embedder, lib/slideshow_risk, lib/source_media_review) can be imported directly OR (cleaner) ported into ClipPilot's own tool layer to map onto the existing ingest->transcribe->find_highlights state machine. The VideoAnalysisBrief shape is a ready-made schema for 'understand the upload like a human'. Claude-as-orchestrator + human-approval-gate + decision-audit-trail + scored 7-dimension provider selection (lib/scoring.py) mirror ClipPilot's locked design and are worth studying. AGPLv3 forces import/ship to be a porting+relicensing or arms-length-service decision, not a casual vendor-in.
reason: Best-in-class CPU-friendly perception stack (video_analyzer fusion + CLIP/scene/face/audio-energy + slideshow-risk) is exactly ClipPilot's missing holistic-understanding piece and is Windows-native, but AGPLv3 + no real emotion/OCR/audio-event classifiers + thin dependency pinning mean you port/adapt selected modules rather than vendor the whole repo.
key_deps: pyyaml>=6.0, pydantic>=2.0, jsonschema>=4.20, python-dotenv>=1.0, Pillow>=10.0, requests>=2.31 (core requirements.txt)
FFmpeg external binary (hard dep for most analysis tools)
Optional/implicit (imported but NOT pinned in requirements): faster-whisper, whisperx, scenedetect[opencv], opencv-python, mediapipe, yt-dlp, youtube-transcript-api, transformers, torch, numpy, scipy
GPU extras (requirements-gpu.txt): torch>=2.0, torchaudio>=2.0, torchvision>=0.15; Makefile install-gpu also adds diffusers/accelerate
Node: remotion (>=18), hyperframes via npx (>=22) -- compose layer only
gotchas: LICENSE = AGPLv3 (network copyleft) - biggest blocker for a commercial closed-source ClipPilot; importing/shipping this code can force you to AGPL your whole app. Port specific modules with legal review or run it as an arms-length service.

Perception deps are NOT pinned: requirements.txt only lists 6 core libs. The whole understanding stack (faster-whisper, transformers, torch, mediapipe, scenedetect, opencv, yt-dlp, youtube-transcript-api, scipy, numpy) is imported lazily and only documented in install_instructions strings -- you must assemble and version-lock these yourself.

video_understand declares runtime=LOCAL_GPU, vram_mb=2048; BLIP-2 (blip2-opt-2.7b) and LLaVA-1.5-7b are impractical on CPU. Only the CLIP path is CPU-viable; for CPU MVP, lean on CLIP + Claude-multimodal for captions/VQA.

No facial-EMOTION model, no audio-event/music-genre classifier, and no OCR for burned-in on-screen text. The repo deliberately offloads 'visual interpretation' to the agent's own vision model -- ClipPilot must supply Claude multimodal for emotion/on-screen-text/energy reads.
mediapipe wheels are version/Python-sensitive on Windows; face_tracker falls back to OpenCV Haar (weaker) if mediapipe is absent.
yt-dlp / youtube-transcript-api third-party sourcing breaks often and raises ToS/guardrail concerns -- fits ClipPilot's 'third-party sourcing with guardrails on' but needs the guardrails.

Agent-first design = there is NO programmatic orchestrator to call; all pipeline logic lives in Markdown skills + YAML manifests meant for an LLM to read. You get tools + schemas, not a turnkey clip engine; ClipPilot's own state machine must drive them.
Many stability flags are EXPERIMENTAL/BETA (video_understand, transcriber, scene_detect, face_tracker, visual_qa all EXPERIMENTAL) -- treat as reference-quality, not production-hardened.

BaseTool.run_command uses check=True and Windows .cmd/.bat resolution (good), but tools shell out to ffmpeg with fixed timeouts (e.g. 60s in video_understand frame extract) that can fail on long uploads.

clippilot_role: Minimal direct role. (1) The whole app is GPL v3 and is a monolithic Qt GUI, not a library/CLI/service — importing or subprocessing it taints ClipPilot's PySide6 app with GPL and it can't run without the external libopenshot binary anyway. (2) Its genuinely useful asset is the PATTERN in classes/comfy_client.py + comfy_templates.py + src/comfyui/*.json: a clean, dependency-light (stdlib-only) client that drives a local ComfyUI server to run Whisper(ASR/SRT), TransNetV2 (scene detect), Depth-Anything, and SAM2. one_liner: Audio-driven lip-sync / talking-head video editing: takes a face video + a target audio (or TTS wav) and re-renders the mouth/lower face to match the new speech, with optional emotion (sad/angry/surprise) and GFPGAN/GPEN face enhancement. Pure generation, not analysis.
language_runtime: Python 3.x (cog.yaml targets 3.11), PyTorch. Heavy reliance on subprocess ffmpeg calls.
license: Apache-2.0 in the repo's own LICENSE file (Apache License Version 2.0 header confirmed). BUT it vendors third_part/GPEN, GFPGAN, face3d (Deep3DFaceRecon + Basel Face Model 3DMM), and ganimation_replicate, several of which carry their own research-only / non-commercial weight licenses not covered by the top-level Apache file.
compute: Needs NVIDIA GPU in practice (multiple stacked nets: DNet/LNet/ENet + Deep3DFace + GPEN + GFPGAN + GANimation per frame). inference.py line 32 has a CPU fallback but GPEN custom CUDA ops are gated to Linux+CUDA only; CPU run is unsupported/extremely slow. ~Several GB VRAM. Definitively NOT a CPU-only MVP fit.

It contributes NOTHING to the new holistic video-understanding feature — every perception module here (face detect, landmarks, 3DMM, parsing) is internal preprocessing for the generator and not exposed; no ASR, scene/shot detection, OCR, object/scene recog, emotion classification of source, music/energy/pacing, or CLIP embeddings.
reason: GPU-only talking-head lip-sync generator that violates the CPU-only MVP constraint and adds zero video-understanding; the dubbing use-case is niche and better served later by a maintained hosted API.
gotchas: GPU-required in practice: GPEN ops (third_part/GPEN/face_model/op/fused_act.py, upfirdn2d.py) JIT-compile CUDA kernels gated by `platform.system()=='Linux' and torch.cuda.is_available()` — on Windows the compiled op path is skipped entirely; whole pipeline is GPU-designed. Conflicts with locked CPU-only MVP.

Windows-hostile deps: dlib==19.24.0 (needs CMake + C++ build toolchain; shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat), basicsr==1.4.2 (notorious for breaking on modern torchvision via removed functional_tensor import), hard pin numpy==1.23.4, librosa==0.9.2.

Apache-2.0 at top level is misleading: vendors GPEN, GFPGAN, Deep3DFaceRecon + Basel Face Model 3DMM (BFM), and ganimation_replicate — several weights are research/non-commercial only. Commercial use (paid clipping/DFY, ad-share) needs a per-component license check before shipping.

No clean library API: inference.py/predict.py rely on sys.path.insert('third_part') hacks, a global args = options() at import time, and a populated checkpoints/ dir (DNet.pt, LNet.pth, ENet.pth, GFPGANv1.3.pth, GPEN-BFR-512, BFM, expression.mat) that must be downloaded separately — only sane integration is subprocess-CLI.
Large vendored tree (third_part/face3d/arcface_torch, GPEN, GFPGAN) — repo is heavy and effectively unmaintained (SIGGRAPH 2022, pins frozen to ~2022 torch/cuda era; cog.yaml uses torch==2.0.1).
subprocess.call uses shell=True on non-Windows and shell=platform.system()!='Windows' inconsistently; ffmpeg must be on PATH.
Outputs deepfake-style synthetic faces — for a third-party-sourcing pipeline with guardrails this is a disclosure/authenticity risk that needs human-approval-gate scrutiny.

ClipPilot is a CPU only MVP with no self hosted GPU video gen and is a clip and repurposing pipeline: ingest existing video, transcribe, find highlights, clip, caption, publish. ViMax is the opposite: it creates net new AI video from a text idea via paid generative APIs and contributes zero video understanding for the new holistic perception feature. Transferable ideas are architectural patterns, not capabilities. First, its agent_runtime loop with OpenAI tool calling, a JSONL event stream over stdio, session_index state, todo and memory tools and a bounded run_shell is a clean reference for how Claude as the MCP brain could drive ClipPilot's state machine. Second, best_image_selector.py shows a vision LLM as judge pattern reusable for thumbnail or frame selection. Optional AI b-roll could be ported via the VideoGenerator protocol but needs paid GPU cloud APIs and breaks the CPU only and no GPU gen MVP guardrail.
reason: It is a generative text to video CREATOR with zero input video understanding; orthogonal to ClipPilot's clip and repurpose pipeline and the new holistic perception need, and depends on paid GPU cloud gen APIs that violate the CPU only and no GPU gen MVP guardrail.

gotchas: No video understanding at all: no ASR, no shot detection on inputs, no object or scene or face or emotion or audio analysis, no Gemini video upload. Does not satisfy ClipPilot's holistic perception requirement.

Generation is fully paid cloud APIs Google Veo 3.1, Yunwu Doubao Seedance, OpenRouter, nano-banana needing API keys and per clip cost; nothing runs locally. Conflicts with CPU only and no self hosted GPU gen MVP scope.
pyproject pins a pytorch-cu128 index for win32 and linux but no module imports torch; dead and misleading dependency config that can confuse uv installs.
requires-python 3.12 strictly; will not install on older Python.
Heavy multimodal deps moviepy, cv2, scenedetect, google-genai are mocked in tests/test_minimax_integration.py, implying brittle install or runtime if a cloud SDK is missing.

Content and IP risk: it fabricates net new AI footage of people, and the bundled main_idea2video.py example prompt is explicitly objectifying; guardrails needed; not a sourcing with attribution model.
Automation surface is the JSONL agent loop in main_agent.py plus a Node tsx TUI; mixing Python and Node adds an extra runtime to ship on Windows.
scenedetect and opencv usage is incidental, a keyframe grab from a generated transition clip, not a reusable input video analyzer.
one_liner:
An LLM agent orchestrated idea, script and novel to video GENERATION pipeline. An OpenAI tool calling agent loop in agent_runtime drives ViMax pipelines that use a LangChain LLM to write story, script, storyboard, shot and camera artifacts, then calls CLOUD image and video APIs such as Google Veo, Yunwu Doubao Seedance, OpenRouter and nano-banana to render keyframes and clips and stitch them with moviepy and ffmpeg. It is a video CREATOR, not a video understander.</one_liner>

clippilot_role: No fit for the CPU-only MVP. ClipPilot's locked decisions explicitly exclude self-hosted GPU video-gen, and every Wan2.2 pipeline hardcodes torch.device('cuda') with 24-80GB VRAM needs - so it cannot run on the target Windows CPU box. It is also a GENERATOR, not a perception tool, so it does NOT serve the new holistic video-understanding requirement (the bundled Qwen2.5-VL and DWPose/SAM2 are internal conditioning helpers, not a usable understanding API). At most a future OPTIONAL B-section faceless b-roll generator via a cloud GPU/managed API (fal/Replicate host Wan2.2), invoked as an external service - not by running this repo locally.
reason: Pure GPU-only (24-80GB VRAM, CUDA-mandatory) video GENERATOR; violates ClipPilot's CPU-only/no-self-hosted-GPU-gen decision and provides no usable video-understanding capability for the new holistic-perception feature.
gotchas: CUDA mandatory: flash_attention() in wan/modules/attention.py hard-asserts q.device.type=='cuda'; all pipeline ctors set torch.device(f'cuda:{device_id}') (text2video.py L72). SDPA fallback exists but the rest of the stack still assumes GPU - no real CPU path.
VRAM: even the 'consumer' TI2V-5B needs ~24GB (RTX 4090) and ~9 min per 5s clip; 14B/A14B/S2V need ~80GB. Far beyond a CPU-only Windows MVP box.
flash_attn dependency is notoriously hard to build on Windows (INSTALL.md documents PEP517 build failures and --no-build-isolation workarounds); effectively WSL/Docker+Linux+CUDA only.
Hard-pinned transformers>=4.49.0,<=4.51.3 and numpy<2 - tight version box likely to clash with other ClipPilot ML deps.
dashscope (Alibaba cloud) baked into prompt_extend / S2V path; the DashScope prompt-extend branch calls an external Alibaba API.

Animate preprocess does `import sam2... ; transformer.OLD_GPU=True` and pulls SAM-2 from a pinned git commit (requirements_animate.txt) + FluxKontextPipeline.to('cuda') - more GPU-only, fragile editable installs.

Generated content + scraped/third-party source video raises provenance/likeness concerns (S2V/Animate create talking-head/deepfake-style output of a reference person) - relevant to ClipPilot's guardrails.
Code is Apache-2.0 but model weights carry a separate license; commercial use of outputs must be checked against the weight license, not the badge.
